evaluation_inputs.system_description: Wargame 2000 is a real-time, human-in-the-loop ballistic missile defense simulation system. It is designed to model concepts of operation, doctrine, and tactics in a realistic combat environment. The system supports distributed discrete event simulation, allowing for the integration of dissimilar parts and interoperability with other simulations via High Level Architecture (HLA).

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Documentation existed in Rational Rose/UML but was considered weak for capturing discrete events. Some architectural drawings caused stakeholder confusion and required real-time refinement.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: How are discrete simulation events represented in the static UML models?
Can you clarify the data flow in this architectural diagram to resolve stakeholder confusion?
How does the code reuse strategy align with the need for high-fidelity discrete event simulation?

hardware_view_description: Distributed simulation environment capable of hosting online, offsite, and multiple simultaneous games.
Networked command centers allowing participants to play from their own locations.
runtime_constraints: Must provide 'Margin' (running faster than real-time) to support future requirements.

questions: How does the client-server separation impact the latency requirements for the human-in-the-loop displays?
How does the publish/subscribe mechanism handle peak load during high-density missile scenarios?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Simulation controller initiates simulation execution, starts subordinate processes, and runs from T=-infinity to T=0.
Change joint firing doctrine rule set in the automated weapon target assigner in time for the next game.
Add cruise missile infrastructure into next software release.
Reconfigure Wargame 2000 from running a theater ballistic missile defense simulation to a national missile defense simulation in one week.
Replace existing database with an object-oriented database within 5-10 staff years.

business_goals: Answer the 'Big Five Questions' regarding operator knowledge and action timing
Serve as a rapid prototyping facility for interoperability studies
Support evolving mission needs and incremental functionality extensions
technical_constraints: Must be compliant with High Level Architecture (HLA) standards
Must run on government-owned contractor-operated infrastructure
Must support human-in-control experiments with 'fog-of-war' realism

architectural_approaches.description: The system employs a client-server separation to support distributed simulation environments with networked command centers, allowing participants to play from their own locations and supporting online, offsite, and multiple simultaneous games.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Enables integration with other simulations via HLA and supports distributed participation across multiple locations.
Client-server separation impacts latency requirements for human-in-the-loop displays, requiring careful management of the 5-second max instantaneous lag and 3-second max average lag constraints.

architectural_approaches.description: Used to handle data distribution and event notification across the distributed simulation environment, particularly during high-density missile scenarios.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Handles peak load during high-density missile scenarios while maintaining real-time deadlines.
Decouples components, allowing for easier integration of new simulation elements and doctrine changes.

architectural_approaches.description: Core architectural pattern supporting the integration of dissimilar simulation parts with timeline fidelity requirements (within 10% of EADSIM events).

addressed_attributes.rationale: Ensures accurate representation of combat scenarios with precise event timing and sequencing.
Allows integration of dissimilar parts and supports incremental functionality extensions.

architectural_approaches.description: Standards-based approach for interoperability with other simulations and systems.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Ensures compatibility with other HLA-compliant simulations and supports rapid prototyping for interoperability studies.
Standardized interfaces facilitate integration of new components and mission capabilities.

children.scenario_description: During normal operations, human-in-the-loop displays must maintain max instantaneous lag < 5 seconds and max average lag < 3 seconds to support operator decision-making.
Simulation controller initiates simulation execution, starts subordinate processes, and completes initialization from T=-infinity to T=0 within 10 minutes.
System runs faster than real-time to provide margin for future requirements and support monthly exercise turnaround frequency.

children.refinement: Peak load handling during high-density scenarios
children.scenario_description: During high-density missile scenario peak load, publish/subscribe mechanism maintains real-time deadlines without degradation.
Change joint firing doctrine rule set in the automated weapon target assigner in time for the next game (within 1 month exercise cycle).
Add cruise missile infrastructure into next software release to support evolving mission needs.
Reconfigure Wargame 2000 from running a theater ballistic missile defense simulation to a national missile defense simulation in one week.
Integrate with other HLA-compliant simulations to serve as rapid prototyping facility for interoperability studies.
Support online, offsite, and multiple simultaneous games with networked command centers allowing participants to play from their own locations.
System maintains statistical adherence to firm real-time deadlines during continuous operation, with data collection window of 30 seconds post-termination.
Distributed discrete event simulation maintains timeline fidelity within 10% of EADSIM events to accurately answer the 'Big Five Questions' regarding operator knowledge and action timing.

scenario_reference.text: During normal operations, human-in-the-loop displays must maintain max instantaneous lag < 5 seconds and max average lag < 3 seconds to support operator decision-making.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with distributed human-in-the-loop displays across networked command centers
analysis_records.stimulus: Continuous stream of simulation events requiring real-time display updates
analysis_records.response_measure: Max instantaneous lag < 5 seconds, max average lag < 3 seconds
architectural_decisions: Client-Server separation with distributed architecture
Publish/Subscribe mechanism for event distribution
Network bandwidth allocation for display updates
analysis_records.reasoning: The client-server architecture enables distributed displays but introduces network latency. The publish/subscribe mechanism must efficiently route display-relevant events to minimize propagation delays. Meeting the 3-second average and 5-second instantaneous lag constraints requires careful network provisioning and event filtering to prevent display clients from being overwhelmed.

risks.description: Network latency variability in distributed environment may cause instantaneous lag to exceed 5-second threshold
risks.rationale: Wide-area network connections to offsite command centers are subject to congestion and routing variability beyond system control, potentially violating hard real-time constraints for human operators

risks.description: Publish/Subscribe event flooding during peak scenarios may overwhelm display clients
risks.rationale: High-density missile scenarios generate massive event volumes; without proper filtering and prioritization, display clients may experience processing backlogs leading to lag accumulation

non_risks.description: Average lag constraint of 3 seconds provides sufficient margin for typical network conditions
non_risks.rationale: The 3-second average allows for occasional spikes while maintaining acceptable operator experience under normal network conditions with proper event prioritization

sensitivity_points.parameter: Network bandwidth between server and distributed clients
Event filtering granularity in publish/subscribe mechanism

scenario_reference.text: Simulation controller initiates simulation execution, starts subordinate processes, and completes initialization from T=-infinity to T=0 within 10 minutes.

analysis_records.environment: System startup with distributed simulation components across multiple nodes
architectural_decisions: Distributed discrete event simulation with coordinated initialization
Client-server architecture requiring process orchestration
HLA compliance with federation initialization protocols
analysis_records.reasoning: The 10-minute initialization window must accommodate starting distributed processes, establishing network connections, synchronizing initial state across all nodes, and completing HLA federation join procedures. The distributed architecture adds complexity compared to monolithic systems but provides necessary scalability.

risks.description: Distributed process startup dependencies may create initialization bottlenecks
risks.rationale: Sequential dependencies in initialization (e.g., server must start before clients) combined with network delays could approach or exceed the 10-minute budget if not carefully orchestrated

non_risks.description: 10-minute window provides adequate margin for typical initialization sequences
non_risks.rationale: Modern distributed systems can typically complete process startup and state synchronization within minutes; the 10-minute budget allows for reasonable fault recovery

sensitivity_points.parameter: Initial state data volume requiring synchronization

scenario_reference.text: System runs faster than real-time to provide margin for future requirements and support monthly exercise turnaround frequency.

analysis_records.environment: Normal simulation execution without human-in-the-loop constraints
architectural_decisions: Distributed discrete event simulation with optimized event processing
Publish/Subscribe mechanism for efficient event routing
Separation of human-in-the-loop displays from core simulation
analysis_records.reasoning: Faster-than-real-time execution requires efficient event processing and minimal synchronization overhead. The distributed architecture must balance parallelism benefits against coordination costs. Decoupling displays allows core simulation to run at maximum speed when human interaction is not required.

risks.description: Distributed synchronization overhead may prevent faster-than-real-time execution
risks.rationale: Discrete event simulation across distributed nodes requires frequent synchronization to maintain causality; this overhead could limit speedup, especially during high-density scenarios with many inter-node dependencies

non_risks.description: Publish/Subscribe decoupling enables core simulation to run independently of display constraints
non_risks.rationale: When displays are not active, the simulation can execute at maximum speed without being throttled by human-in-the-loop lag requirements

sensitivity_points.parameter: Event processing capacity per simulation node

scenario_reference.text: During high-density missile scenario peak load, publish/subscribe mechanism maintains real-time deadlines without degradation.

analysis_records.environment: Peak load conditions with high-density missile scenario generating maximum event volume
analysis_records.stimulus: Massive simultaneous missile launches creating event storm
architectural_decisions: Publish/Subscribe mechanism with scalable event routing
Event prioritization and filtering strategies
Distributed processing to handle peak loads
analysis_records.reasoning: High-density missile scenarios represent worst-case load conditions. The publish/subscribe mechanism must scale to handle event storms while maintaining real-time responsiveness. This requires efficient routing algorithms, adequate network bandwidth, and possibly event aggregation or filtering to prevent subscriber overload.

risks.description: Publish/Subscribe broker may become bottleneck during peak event storms
risks.rationale: Centralized or insufficiently distributed pub/sub infrastructure could saturate under high-density missile scenario loads, causing event queuing delays that violate real-time deadlines

risks.description: Network bandwidth saturation during peak loads may cause event delivery delays
risks.rationale: High-density scenarios generate correlated event bursts that could exceed network capacity, particularly on links to distributed command centers

non_risks.description: Event filtering and prioritization can reduce load on non-critical subscribers
non_risks.rationale: Publish/Subscribe pattern allows selective event delivery; subscribers can filter irrelevant events, reducing processing load during peak scenarios

scenario_reference.text: Change joint firing doctrine rule set in the automated weapon target assigner in time for the next game (within 1 month exercise cycle).

analysis_records.environment: Development and testing environment between exercises
analysis_records.stimulus: Request to modify firing doctrine rules
analysis_records.response_measure: Complete modification within 1 month exercise cycle
architectural_decisions: Modular rule-based architecture for doctrine representation
Separation of doctrine rules from core simulation logic
Testing framework for validating rule changes
analysis_records.reasoning: Meeting the 1-month cycle requires doctrine rules to be externalized from core code, ideally in declarative format. The publish/subscribe mechanism supports loose coupling between doctrine components and simulation. Testing must be efficient to validate changes within the time budget.

risks.description: Doctrine rule changes may have unforeseen interactions with other simulation components
risks.rationale: Complex rule sets can have non-obvious dependencies; insufficient testing time within the 1-month cycle could allow defects to reach production exercises

non_risks.description: 1-month cycle provides adequate time for isolated rule set modifications
non_risks.rationale: If doctrine rules are properly modularized and externalized, simple rule changes can be implemented and tested within weeks

sensitivity_points.parameter: Degree of coupling between doctrine rules and core simulation

scenario_reference.text: Add cruise missile infrastructure into next software release to support evolving mission needs.

analysis_records.environment: Development environment for major capability addition
analysis_records.stimulus: Requirement to add new weapon system type
analysis_records.response_measure: Complete addition within next software release cycle
architectural_decisions: HLA compliance for standardized component interfaces
Publish/Subscribe mechanism for loose coupling
Distributed discrete event simulation supporting heterogeneous entities
analysis_records.reasoning: Adding cruise missile infrastructure requires new entity types, behaviors, and interactions. HLA compliance provides standardized interfaces for integration. The publish/subscribe mechanism allows new components to subscribe to relevant events without modifying existing publishers. The discrete event simulation framework must accommodate new event types.

risks.description: Cruise missile addition may require modifications to multiple existing components
risks.rationale: New weapon systems often interact with detection, tracking, engagement, and damage assessment components; ripple effects could extend development time beyond release cycle

risks.description: Performance impact of cruise missile simulation may not be predictable until integration
risks.rationale: Cruise missiles have different flight profiles and engagement timelines than ballistic missiles; computational load and event generation patterns may stress the system differently

non_risks.description: HLA compliance facilitates integration of new entity types
non_risks.rationale: Standardized HLA interfaces and object models provide clear integration points for new simulation components

sensitivity_points.parameter: Modularity of existing weapon system implementations

scenario_reference.text: Reconfigure Wargame 2000 from running a theater ballistic missile defense simulation to a national missile defense simulation in one week.

analysis_records.stimulus: Request to change mission scope from theater to national defense
architectural_decisions: Parameterized scenario configuration
Modular component architecture allowing substitution
Separation of scenario data from simulation logic
analysis_records.reasoning: The 1-week reconfiguration requirement is extremely aggressive for such a major scope change. This demands that theater vs. national differences be primarily configuration-driven rather than code-driven. Geographic scope, sensor coverage, weapon inventories, and threat characteristics must be parameterizable.

risks.description: One week is insufficient for major architectural changes required by theater-to-national transition
risks.rationale: Theater and national missile defense have fundamentally different scales, timelines, and engagement geometries; if these differences require code changes rather than configuration, 1 week is unrealistic

risks.description: Testing and validation cannot be thorough within 1-week timeframe
risks.rationale: Major reconfigurations introduce risk of subtle errors; 1 week allows minimal testing, potentially compromising exercise validity

sensitivity_points.parameter: Degree of parameterization vs. hard-coding of mission scope

scenario_reference.text: Integrate with other HLA-compliant simulations to serve as rapid prototyping facility for interoperability studies.

analysis_records.stimulus: Request to integrate with external HLA simulation
analysis_records.response_measure: Successful federation establishment and data exchange
architectural_decisions: HLA compliance with standardized FOM/SOM
Publish/Subscribe mechanism mapping to HLA RTI
Standardized object and interaction models
analysis_records.reasoning: HLA compliance is the primary architectural decision enabling this scenario. The system's internal publish/subscribe mechanism must map cleanly to HLA's RTI services. Standardized Federation Object Model (FOM) and Simulation Object Model (SOM) definitions are critical for semantic interoperability.

risks.description: Semantic mismatches between FOMs may require custom translation logic
risks.rationale: While HLA provides syntactic interoperability, different simulations may model entities and interactions differently, requiring mapping logic that undermines rapid prototyping goals

non_risks.description: HLA RTI handles low-level communication and synchronization
non_risks.rationale: HLA infrastructure provides proven mechanisms for distributed simulation coordination, reducing integration effort

sensitivity_points.parameter: Degree of FOM/SOM standardization across federates

scenario_reference.text: Support online, offsite, and multiple simultaneous games with networked command centers allowing participants to play from their own locations.

analysis_records.environment: Distributed gaming environment with multiple concurrent exercises
analysis_records.stimulus: Multiple simultaneous exercises with geographically distributed participants
analysis_records.response_measure: Successful multi-game operation with participant connectivity
architectural_decisions: Client-Server architecture with distributed clients
Network infrastructure supporting multiple concurrent sessions
Session isolation and resource partitioning
analysis_records.reasoning: Supporting multiple simultaneous games requires session isolation to prevent cross-contamination while sharing infrastructure. The client-server architecture must scale to support multiple server instances or multi-tenant operation. Network design must accommodate concurrent games without interference.

risks.description: Resource contention between simultaneous games may cause performance degradation
risks.rationale: Multiple concurrent exercises compete for server CPU, memory, and network bandwidth; without proper resource allocation, one game could impact others

risks.description: Network partitions could isolate distributed participants from their game session
risks.rationale: Wide-area network failures could disconnect offsite participants; recovery mechanisms must handle reconnection without disrupting exercise flow

non_risks.description: Client-server separation naturally supports distributed participation
non_risks.rationale: The architectural pattern inherently accommodates remote clients connecting over networks

scenario_reference.text: System maintains statistical adherence to firm real-time deadlines during continuous operation, with data collection window of 30 seconds post-termination.

analysis_records.stimulus: Ongoing simulation execution with firm deadlines
analysis_records.response_measure: Statistical adherence to real-time deadlines (measured over 30-second windows)
architectural_decisions: Distributed discrete event simulation with deadline scheduling
Real-time operating system or priority scheduling
Monitoring and measurement infrastructure
analysis_records.reasoning: Firm real-time deadlines require predictable execution with bounded latency. The distributed architecture complicates this by introducing network delays and synchronization points. Statistical adherence (rather than absolute guarantees) suggests the system can tolerate occasional deadline misses but must maintain overall performance.

risks.description: Distributed synchronization may cause unpredictable deadline violations
risks.rationale: Network delays and remote node failures can cause cascading deadline misses that exceed acceptable statistical thresholds

non_risks.description: Statistical adherence allows for occasional transient overloads
non_risks.rationale: The 30-second measurement window permits short-term deadline misses as long as overall performance meets requirements

sensitivity_points.parameter: Network latency variance between distributed nodes

conflicting_attributes: Performance (soft deadlines allow better average throughput)

scenario_reference.text: Distributed discrete event simulation maintains timeline fidelity within 10% of EADSIM events to accurately answer the 'Big Five Questions' regarding operator knowledge and action timing.

analysis_records.environment: Combat scenario simulation requiring high-fidelity event timing
analysis_records.stimulus: Complex engagement scenarios with precise timing requirements
analysis_records.response_measure: Timeline accuracy within 10% of EADSIM reference
architectural_decisions: Distributed discrete event simulation with synchronized clocks
Event ordering and causality preservation mechanisms
High-resolution timing infrastructure
analysis_records.reasoning: The 10% fidelity requirement demands precise event timing across distributed nodes. Clock synchronization, event ordering, and causality preservation are critical. The distributed architecture introduces challenges because events on different nodes must maintain correct temporal relationships despite network delays.

risks.description: Clock drift between distributed nodes may accumulate beyond 10% tolerance
risks.rationale: Without robust clock synchronization (e.g., NTP, PTP), independent node clocks will drift, causing timeline divergence that violates fidelity requirements

risks.description: Network delays may cause event reordering that violates causality
risks.rationale: Events generated on different nodes may arrive out of order due to variable network delays, potentially causing incorrect simulation outcomes

non_risks.description: 10% tolerance provides reasonable margin for distributed timing variations
non_risks.rationale: The tolerance allows for small timing discrepancies inherent in distributed systems while maintaining sufficient accuracy for operator decision analysis
